symbols: great fish or whale — the instrument of divine discipline and preservation, the belly of death from which God delivers
churning sea and waves — chaos, divine judgment, and the overwhelming power of God over creation
ship — the vessel of Jonah's attempted flight from God, human plans overwhelmed by divine will
swirling, turbulent composition — the disorder and terror of resisting the call of God
darkness of the print — the abyss, Sheol, the depths into which Jonah descends before his deliverance
small human figure amid vast forces — the helplessness of man before the sovereignty of God
description: A black-and-white woodcut or linocut print of intense dynamism and expressionistic energy. The composition is dominated by swirling, curving forms suggesting massive waves, a coiling sea creature, and turbulent water. At lower center, a small human figure appears caught within or being swallowed by the enormous spiraling body of a whale or sea monster. Above and around, jagged diagonal lines suggest driving rain, wind, and spray. The fragments of what may be a ship's hull are visible amid the chaos. A small circular form in the upper right may represent the moon or sun.
theological_description: James Lesesne Wells's expressionistic rendering of Jonah and the Whale captures the terrifying power of God's sovereign pursuit of His reluctant prophet. The Book of Jonah (Jonah 1-2) recounts how God commanded Jonah to preach repentance to Nineveh, but the prophet fled by sea in the opposite direction. God sent a great storm, and the sailors, learning Jonah was the cause, cast him overboard, whereupon the Lord appointed a great fish to swallow him. The swirling, chaotic energy of this print embodies the theological truth that no creature can flee from the presence of God (Psalm 139:7-10). The overwhelming forces of sea and beast are not enemies of the divine plan but instruments of it — God uses the very elements of chaos to accomplish His purposes of mercy. Jonah's three days in the belly of the fish became, in Christ's own teaching, the supreme Old Testament type of His death, burial, and resurrection: 'For just as Jonah was three days and three nights in the belly of the great fish, so will the Son of Man be three days and three nights in the heart of the earth' (Matthew 12:40). The descent into the deep thus becomes a descent into death itself, from which God alone can bring deliverance. Jonah's prayer from inside the fish (Jonah 2) is one of Scripture's most powerful testimonies to God's faithfulness in the uttermost extremity: 'Out of the belly of Sheol I cried, and you heard my voice.'

scripture_refs.reason: Jonah's flight from God, the storm at sea, and his being swallowed by the great fish — the scene directly depicted
Jonah's prayer from the belly of the fish — the theological heart of the scene, the cry from the depths
Christ declares Jonah's three days in the fish as the sign prefiguring His own death and resurrection
Where can I flee from your presence? — the impossibility of escaping God, demonstrated in Jonah's failed flight
The waters have come up to my neck, I sink in deep mire — the imagery of overwhelming waters parallels Jonah's descent
Deep calls to deep at the roar of your waterfalls — the swirling chaos of the sea as an image of divine overwhelming power
